candidates.evidence_quote: Over the past several years we have experienced and continue to experience significant labor cost inflation, which has and may continue to significantly increase our cost of doing business.
The aggregate impact of these and other geopolitical and macroeconomic factors contributed to significantly increased commodity and wage inflation and other increased costs.

we began to experience certain supply shortages and transportation delays largely attributable to impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic. These shortages continued in fiscal 2022 and were exacerbated by geopolitical unrest.
We utilize menu price increases in an effort to offset inflation of key operating costs. However, our menu price increases may be insufficient to meaningfully offset increased costs
unfavorable menu mix shifts (i.e. customers reducing their spend by purchasing fewer menu items or lower cost menu items)

Any factor affecting consumer discretionary spending may influence customer traffic in our restaurants and average check amount, thus potentially having a material impact on our financial performance.
We strive to increase comparable restaurant sales by improving customer traffic trends and growing average check.
Our inability to grow comparable restaurant sales could materially adversely affect our financial performance.
The impact global and domestic economic conditions have on consumer discretionary spending could materially adversely affect our financial performance.
In recent years we have generated a higher mix of sales from off-premise channels as consumers have increasingly demonstrated a preference for convenience and at-home dining due to COVID-19.
We compete directly and indirectly for customer traffic with national and regional casual dining restaurant chains, as well as independently-owned restaurants.

Our greatest asset is the value of our brands, which is directly linked to our reputation. We must protect our reputation in order to continue to be successful and to grow the value of our brands domestically and internationally.

We also face food safety risks, including the risk of food-borne illness and food contamination (including allergen cross contamination), which are common both in the restaurant industry and the food supply chain.
We utilize a web-based solution to efficiently contact our restaurants and monitor progress in the event of a product withdrawal or recall.

The COVID-19 pandemic significantly disrupted our business and its ongoing effects and other geopolitical and macroeconomic events are having and may continue to have an adverse impact on our operating environment.
While most of our restaurants operated with no restrictions on indoor dining during fiscal years 2021 and 2022, our revenues were negatively impacted during periods of accelerating case counts
we incurred increased restaurant staff absenteeism and temporary shifts in consumer behavior, such as changes in customer traffic or the mix between on-premise and off-premise channels
we experienced labor shortfalls relative to our sales levels in certain parts of our workforce and/or geographies as we reopened our dining rooms

We have also encountered delays in opening new restaurants due to supply chain challenges, as well as to longer lead times in obtaining licenses and permits and in the timeline required to complete the overall leasing process with landlords.
the number and timing of new restaurant openings and associated preopening costs

Health care costs continue to rise and are especially difficult to project given that material increases in costs associated with medical claims, or an increase in the severity or frequency of such claims, may cause health care costs to vary substantially from quarter-to-quarter and year-over-year.
We believe it is becoming increasing likely that the United States federal government will significantly increase the federal minimum wage and tip credit wage (or eliminate the tip credit wage)
In addition to increasing the overall wages paid to our minimum wage and tip credit wage earners, these increases create pressure to increase wages and other benefits paid to other staff members
Low unemployment coupled with increases in minimum wages and minimum tip credit wages, extensions of personal and other leave policies, other governmental regulations affecting labor costs
reduced levels of legal immigration and a diminishing pool of potential staff members, which has been exacerbated by potential staff members choosing to exit the workforce
Any failure of our third-party delivery provider to represent our brands in a favorable manner could damage our reputation.
we work with several premiere third-party gift card distributors, contributing to our brand awareness and gift card sales
We also are currently testing a guest rewards program pilot for potential national rollout in 2023.
Dram shop litigation may result in significant judgments, including punitive damages.

We employ a multi-discipline security approach to recognize, manage and resolve cybersecurity threats, and require cybersecurity awareness training for all staff members with access to our cyber systems.
we may again implement mitigation actions such as suspending share repurchases, not declaring future dividends, increasing borrowings under our credit facility or modifying our operating strategies
we may again implement mitigation actions such as suspending share repurchases, not declaring future dividends, increasing borrowings under our credit facility or modifying our operating strategies
we may again implement mitigation actions such as suspending share repurchases, not declaring future dividends, increasing borrowings under our credit facility or modifying our operating strategies
macroeconomic conditions that impact consumer discretionary spending (such as significant cost inflation and interest rates above recent historical norms)

year-over-year comparisons can be significantly impacted by factors such as significant differences in year-over-year inflation, the number and timing of new restaurant openings and associated preopening costs, the timing of holidays, inclement weather
the timing of holidays, inclement weather and the additional week in a 53-week fiscal year

notes: Chunk has fiscal_kpis=null (KPIs live on chunk_001); all candidates come from the single event: 10-K 0001104659-23-026072 (2023-02-27), part 2 of 6 (business overview tail + Risk Factors head).

Skipped as vague/non-causal/descriptive (12): geopolitical unrest (bare category); consumer confidence/credit conditions/home values/stock market laundry list (folded into consumer_discretionary_spending); dairy/poultry contract mentions (no causal attribution to results); alcoholic beverage control regulations (descriptive compliance); data privacy regulation (descriptive compliance); FICA tip tax assessments (narrow conditional); staff retention trends (BI dashboard mention, non-causal); HR programs incl. pay equity/training/benefits/earned wage access (descriptive); sustainability and food donation programs (descriptive); 53-week extra operating week (period token, unnameable under rules); media impressions/social media marketing strategy (descriptive); international licensee and CPG licensing marketing (descriptive).

Naming notes: 'comparable restaurant sales' mapped to standard whole phrase same_store_sales; 'delays in opening new restaurants' named new_restaurant_development to avoid banned state word 'opening'; commodity_inflation and labor_cost_inflation kept separate (input costs vs wages); wage-inflation mentions deduped into labor_cost_inflation; share_repurchases/dividend_payments/credit_facility_borrowings share one mitigation-sentence quote per R2 (one driver per causal variable).
